content:
What schema markup means, in plain terms for everyone — and what to do if you don’t know how to code
WHAT IS SCHEMA MARKUP?
Schema markup is an invisible label you add to your site to tell Google directly what your page contains. You don’t see it when you browse the site. Google does. With it, Google instantly understands: this is a product, a clinic, an article, or a list of questions. Without it, Google has to guess — and sometimes it guesses wrong.
You don’t need to know how to code to use it. Below, I’ll show you exactly how to proceed, step by step
WHY SHOULD YOU CARE ABOUT THIS
Picture two results in Google, for the same search.
The first: a plain link, a title, two lines of gray text.
The second: the same type of link, but with star ratings, a small photo, the service price, and a dropdown with 3 frequently asked questions right underneath it.
Which one would you click?
The second result exists because that site has schema markup. Your site can look the same way — but only if it has the right label built into the code.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A side-by-side look at what schema markup changes in the search results. Image created with AI at the author’s request.WHAT IS “STRUCTURED DATA,” EXACTLY?
Think of the label on a box at the store. The box clearly states: product name, weight, price, expiration date. Anyone looking at it understands immediately, without opening the box.
Structured data does exactly that, but for a web page. Instead of Google having to “open the box” and try to interpret your text, you place a clear label on top of it: “This is a service. It’s called Consultation. It costs 150 lei. It’s offered by Clinic X.”
The standard used for these labels is called Schema.org. It’s not a Google invention. It’s maintained jointly by Google, Microsoft, Yahoo, and Yandex — precisely so it works the same way everywhere.
Source: Schema.org — the official documentation itself defines this standard as a set of schemas through which any site can explicitly describe its content for search engines and other applications that read the web.
AND WHAT IS JSON-LD? WHY DOES IT ALWAYS COME UP NEXT TO SCHEMA MARKUP?
JSON-LD is just the format in which the label is written. It’s the technical language of the label, not the concept itself.
You don’t need to learn it. You just need to know one thing: Google officially recommends JSON-LD as the format, because it has the lowest chance of errors. When a plugin or a tool “generates schema markup,” it’s actually writing this JSON-LD code automatically, on your behalf.
So whenever you hear “JSON-LD,” mentally translate it as: “the standard format, generated automatically, that I don’t need to touch.”
IS SCHEMA MARKUP A DIRECT RANKING FACTOR IN GOOGLE?
No. Google has publicly confirmed, many times, that schema markup doesn’t directly raise your site’s position.
It does, however, have an indirect effect, through two channels:

• Higher CTR — a result with stars, a price, or an FAQ attracts more clicks than a plain link, even if its position on the page stays the same

• Better page understanding — helps Google correctly classify your content, which can help indirectly over the long term
Source: Google Search Central — Google’s official documentation shows exactly what types of visual results you can get through correctly implemented structured data, from star ratings to breadcrumbs.
DOES SCHEMA MARKUP HELP YOU APPEAR IN AI OVERVIEW?
This is more nuanced than what’s usually claimed online — and it’s worth knowing the truth, not just the promise.
Source: Ahrefs — the Ahrefs team tested this exact question on 1,885 pages that had schema markup added, compared with pages that didn’t. Their conclusion: adding schema markup, on its own, did not lead to a clear increase in citations across Google AI Overviews, AI Mode, or ChatGPT.
Why? Because many AI systems, when accessing a page in real time, only read the visible text. They ignore the hidden code behind it.
What does this mean, practically, for you?
Don’t implement schema markup thinking it will automatically get you into ChatGPT’s or Gemini’s answers. That’s not how it works. But it’s still worth implementing, because:

• it helps you appear with rich snippets in classic Google search

• it helps Google index and understand you correctly

• it builds a clear brand identity (who you are, what you offer), useful in the long run
AN EXAMPLE FROM MY OWN PRACTICE
In a full SEO audit for a dental clinic, I found that the site had good content — carefully written text, accurate information — but zero schema markup.
Google had no way of knowing, from the code, that this was a dental practice, with a specific schedule, with specific services. All that information existed only in text, for the human eye.
I recommended implementing three types of schema: one for the business’s identity, one for location and hours, and one for the FAQ section. None of this required me to write any code — I specified exactly what information needed to be included, and the technical team implemented the code.
That’s actually the role of an SEO specialist in this process: you don’t write the code, but you know exactly what it needs to contain and why.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A real example from an SEO audit: strong content, but no machine-readable structure behind it. Image created with AI at the author’s request.WHICH SCHEMA MARKUP TYPES ACTUALLY MATTER
You don’t need every type that exists. You only need the ones suited to your site.

• Organization → Brand identity: name, logo, contact info → Entity recognition, knowledge panel

• LocalBusiness → Businesses with a physical location (clinics, stores) → Appearance in maps and local results

• Article / BlogPosting → Blog articles → Author, date, image in results

• Product → Product pages → Price, stock, rating directly in the SERP

• FAQPage → Frequently asked questions sections → Expanded dropdown directly in Google

• BreadcrumbList → Your site’s structure → Breadcrumb trail instead of a raw URL
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The six schema types worth prioritizing, and what each one unlocks in search. Image created with AI at the author’s request.Simple rule for a new site: start with Organization. It’s the foundation. Only then add the type specific to each page.
HOW TO IMPLEMENT SCHEMA MARKUP WITHOUT KNOWING HOW TO CODE
You have three possible paths. The choice depends on how your site is built.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Three no-code paths to implementing schema markup, depending on your site setup. Image created with AI at the author’s request.Path 1: You have a WordPress site → use a plugin
The simplest option. The plugin does almost all the work; you just fill in fields on a form.

• Rank Math (free, with a paid PRO version) — has a “wizard” that asks what type of business you have and automatically generates the appropriate schema. Very beginner-friendly for someone who doesn’t code.

• Yoast SEO (free, with a paid Premium version) — automatically generates basic schema (Organization, Article, BreadcrumbList) just from the site’s settings, without you doing anything extra.
Path 2: You don’t have WordPress → use an online generator
You fill in a simple form (name, address, hours, price), and the tool writes ready-to-use JSON-LD code for you. You don’t touch the code — you just copy it and pass it along.

• Google Structured Data Markup Helper (free) — Google’s official tool. You select the page type, highlight the information on the page with your mouse, and it generates the code.

• Dentsu’s Schema Markup Generator (free) — a very popular generator in the SEO community, simple to fill in, with separate forms for each schema type (product, article, local business, FAQ).
Path 3: Custom site or a large number of pages → hire a specialist
If your site has hundreds of pages, or is built manually (not on a platform like WordPress), the safest option is to work with a developer or an agency. You, as an SEO specialist or business owner, handle the strategy: which schema type, which pages, which information. The developer implements the actual code.
After implementation: always validate
Whichever path you take, the last step is always the same: verify that the schema works correctly, using the Rich Results Test, Google’s free tool. Enter the page’s URL, and it will tell you whether everything is valid or something is missing.
THE MOST COMMON MISTAKES

• The information in the schema doesn’t match what’s visible on the page. If the code says the price is 100 lei, but the site shows 150 lei, Google may stop displaying the rich result.

• Using FAQPage schema on content that isn’t really an FAQ. It needs to be genuine questions and answers, not section headings disguised as questions.

• Forgetting to update the schema when you update the site. You change the clinic’s hours, but forget to change the information in the code too.

• Not validating the code before publishing. A small error can invalidate the entire block, with no visible signal on the site.
FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
Do I need to know how to code to use schema markup?
No. You can use a plugin (if you have WordPress), a free online generator, or hire a developer to implement the code for you.
Does schema markup directly increase ranking?
No. Google has confirmed it’s not a direct ranking factor. It can influence rankings indirectly, through better CTR and clearer page understanding.
Which schema type should I implement first?
Organization. It’s the foundation of your online identity. Only after that should you add specific types, such as Product or FAQPage.
Does schema markup help with AI Overview?
Partially and indirectly. A 2026 Ahrefs study shows that adding it alone doesn’t clearly increase citations in AI-generated answers. It’s still useful for indexing and identity clarity, though.
Is it free to implement schema markup?
Yes, it can be completely free — using a free plugin or a free online generator. Paid options (PRO plugins, agencies) offer more convenience and implementation support for complex sites, but they’re not mandatory.
WHAT TO DO, STARTING TODAY
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A five-step starting checklist — save this one for later. Image created with AI at the author’s request
• Check whether your site already has schema markup — enter the URL into Google’s Rich Results Test.

• If you don’t have it, decide which path to take: plugin (WordPress), online generator (custom site), or specialist (large number of pages).

• Start with the Organization type — the basic information about your business.

• Then add the type specific to your main pages — Product, Article, or LocalBusiness.

• Always validate before publishing.
You don’t need to do everything today. You just need to take the first step correctly.
Have you tried any of the tools on the list — Rank Math, Yoast, or an online generator? Let me know in the comments which one you found easiest to use.
If this article helped you understand the topic, follow me on Medium so you don’t miss the next guide in this series.
